hot_topics.topic: Trump's US-Iran Agreement Sparks Controversy and Conflict Analysis
hot_topics.synthesis: A preliminary agreement between the US and Iran, signed by President Trump, aims to address tensions but faces legal challenges regarding Trump's authority to waive sanctions. While some see it as a strategic move, others view it as a concession to Iran, with reports of thousands killed in a US-Israeli war on Iran and experts debating the deal's lasting viability.

hot_topics.topic: Major Shifts and Investments in the AI Sector, Including OpenAI and Amazon
hot_topics.synthesis: The AI industry is experiencing significant changes, with OpenAI seeing executive departures and new high-profile hires ahead of its IPO, while Amazon Web Services plans to sell its AI chips to directly compete with Nvidia. Startups like Baseten are raising massive funding rounds, reflecting an "inference gold rush," and AI is being integrated into diverse applications from telecom services to wellness apps.

hot_topics.topic: Juneteenth Commemorations Highlight History and Ongoing Quest for Freedom
hot_topics.synthesis: Juneteenth is being commemorated with reflections on its historical significance, marking the delayed news of emancipation to enslaved people in Texas, and its continued relevance to America's enduring pursuit of freedom and democracy. Celebrations occur alongside personal announcements and discussions on progressive patriotism.

hot_topics.topic: Donald Trump's Enduring Influence on Domestic and International Politics
hot_topics.synthesis: Donald Trump continues to shape political narratives both domestically and internationally, from his alleged fabricated claims at the G7 summit to influencing local elections and being discussed in relation to the Democratic Party's future. Even trivial matters like the D.C. Reflecting Pool's unexpected color change are inadvertently linked to his past visions.

items.title: Trauma creep
items.summary: The essay "Trauma creep" argues that the term "trauma" has lost its meaning due to overuse, yet it describes a real phenomenon that needs to be reclaimed. It advocates for precision in language to accurately convey the concept of trauma.

items.title: Android verification is coming: Google confirms timeline and supported app stores
items.summary: Google is rolling out a contentious developer verification system for Android app stores, starting September 30 in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand. This aims to combat malware apps by verifying developer identities outside the Play Store, a move some see as creating a "walled garden."

items.title: As China looms, Taiwan makes more drones for defense and the US military
items.summary: Taiwan is significantly boosting its domestic military drone production with a proposed $6.6 billion budget over six years to deter a potential Chinese invasion. The effort includes acquiring over 208,000 coastal attack drones and 1,400 reconnaissance drones, alongside international partnerships for sales to the US military.

items.title: As global warming threatens corals, scientists search for reefs that can take the heat
items.summary: Scientists are using a robot, Yellowfin, to search for coral reefs resilient to global warming in the Central Pacific, led by researcher Anne Cohen. This effort aims to identify corals that can withstand rising ocean temperatures.

items.title: FDA advisors unanimously vote to approve Moderna's mRNA after agency drama
items.summary: FDA advisors unanimously recommended approving Moderna's seasonal mRNA flu vaccine, mRNA-1010 (mFlusiva), despite earlier attempts by an agency appointee to block its review. Trials showed the vaccine was more effective than standard flu shots and produced stronger immune responses in older adults, with a good safety profile.

items.title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
items.summary: Microsoft has identified a new self-propagating worm, "Crypto Clipper," that spreads via USB drives to steal cryptocurrency credentials and screenshots, sending them to attackers via Tor and a SOCKS5 proxy. This lightweight backdoor operates without a traditional installer or exposed IP-based infrastructure.

items.title: Asia's richest man Ambani announces what could be India's biggest share sale
items.summary: Asia's richest man, Mukesh Ambani, has announced what could be India's largest share sale, with his telecom operator expected to raise around $4 billion. This significant financial move underscores the scale of Reliance's expansion and investment.

items.title: Boy, 12, wins hearts after trying to check sick chicken into Ethiopian hospital
items.summary: A 12-year-old boy in Ethiopia won hearts after attempting to check his sick hen into a human hospital when traditional treatments failed. The story highlights the child's deep affection for his pet and his desperate attempt to save it.

items.title: Do it at home too, women tell Japanese fans who cleaned World Cup stadium
items.summary: Japanese women are urging men to apply the same cleaning diligence at home as they exhibit cleaning World Cup stadiums, highlighting a double standard in household chores. This points to ongoing gender role discussions in Japan.

items.title: In Trump's shadow, Vance becomes face of Iran deal
items.summary: Amid rising criticism, JD Vance has become a leading defender of the Iran deal, intensifying speculation about a potential 2028 presidential bid. His prominent role highlights the political implications of the agreement.

items.title: Italy's Meloni says Trump 'made up' story that she 'begged' him for photo at G7
items.summary: Italy's Giorgia Meloni stated that Donald Trump "made up" a story claiming she "begged" him for a photo at the G7 summit. This public disagreement suggests a deterioration of their previously close relationship, possibly linked to Trump's decision to go to war with Iran.

items.title: Mangione's lawyers reverse course on psychiatric defence in state murder trial
items.summary: Mangione's lawyers have reversed their decision to pursue a psychiatric defense in his state murder trial, a day after initially stating they would argue "extreme emotional disturbance." This change in strategy signals a shift in the legal approach.

items.title: Morocco captain Achraf Hakimi to stand trial for rape
items.summary: Morocco captain Achraf Hakimi will stand trial for rape, French prosecutors confirmed, based on accusations from 2023. This development brings the high-profile case to a formal legal proceeding.

items.title: Obama moved to tears by wife Michelle's speech
items.summary: Barack Obama was moved to tears by Michelle Obama's speech during the opening of a new site in Chicago, which the former president described as a "celebration of community." The event highlighted their ongoing commitment to public service and community building.

items.title: Parents of Serbia's teenage school shooter given jail terms in retrial
items.summary: The parents of a 13-year-old Serbian school shooter have been given jail terms in a retrial for the 2023 massacre that killed nine people. This legal outcome addresses the responsibility surrounding the tragic event.

items.title: Thirty-five killed as gunmen attack Niger's biggest airport
items.summary: Gunmen attacked Niger's largest airport, killing 35 people, marking the second such incident in January. Niger has been battling a militant Islamist insurgency for a decade, with this attack highlighting ongoing security challenges.

items.title: Thousands killed in US-Israeli war on Iran - but experts say true total may never be known
items.summary: Experts state that thousands have been killed in the US-Israeli war on Iran, but the true casualty count may never be known due to internet, media, and government restrictions. These limitations hamper accurate reporting across the conflict-affected region.

items.title: UK actress charged with importing meth worth almost A$300m into Australia
items.summary: UK actress Emaa Hussen, 34, has been charged with importing nearly A$300 million worth of methamphetamines into Australia, hidden in charcoal bags. She faces a potential life sentence if convicted.

items.title: What Iran and US get from deal and why both could struggle to keep it
items.summary: BBC analysts are assessing what both Washington and Tehran gain from the deal to end their war, and why both sides might struggle to maintain it. Each side claims victory, but the agreement faces significant challenges.

items.title: Zimbabwe MPs pass bill to extend president's time in power
items.summary: Zimbabwe MPs have passed a bill that would extend President Emmerson Mnangagwa's term by two years and eliminate direct presidential elections. This move raises concerns about democratic processes and presidential term limits.

items.title: Project Valhalla, Explained: How a Decade of Work Arrives in JDK 28
items.summary: Project Valhalla, a decade-long development effort, is set to be integrated into JDK 28, bringing significant architectural changes to the Java platform. The project aims to improve performance and memory efficiency through value types.

items.title: The AirPods Effect
items.summary: The article discusses "The AirPods Effect," exploring the broader impact of Apple's AirPods on consumer behavior and the tech industry. It delves into how a single product can redefine a market and create new user expectations.

items.title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
items.summary: "Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP" details an enterprise-managed authentication solution designed for the Model Context Protocol. This approach simplifies OAuth implementation for large organizations, enhancing security and streamlining access.

items.title: Big dogs, Buc-ee's and the great BBQ debate: World Cup fans discover everyday America
items.summary: World Cup visitors to the US are discovering aspects of everyday American life like free drink refills and Buc-ee's, as tensions between Washington and its allies are high. Their experiences offer a respite amidst the geopolitical climate.

items.title: Get with the times — here's what a 'Luddite' means today
items.summary: NPR's Word of the Week reexamines the term "Luddite," often used derogatorily for technophobes, revealing its historical context where Luddites were not simply backwards-looking but protested technological disruption. The discussion also touches on AI's implications today.

items.title: How and why algae clouded Trump's vision for an 'American flag blue' Reflecting Pool
items.summary: The newly refurbished Reflecting Pool in D.C. turned green due to algae growth, which thrives in shallow, sunny waters, with experts suggesting the recent renovation may have accelerated the issue. This phenomenon overshadowed Trump's reported vision for an "American flag blue" pool.

items.title: How the 1874 Freedman's Bank collapse connects to economic disparities we see today
items.summary: Historian Justene Hill Edwards' book "Savings and Trust" explores the 1874 collapse of the Freedman's Bank, established for formerly enslaved people, and its connection to current economic disparities. The book sheds light on historical financial injustices and their lasting impact.

items.title: It's toys vs. tech in 'Toy Story 5.' Here are 4 ways to keep tech in check this summer
items.summary: With "Toy Story 5" portraying problematic screen use, parents are given tips to help children manage technology and enjoy real-life activities over the summer. Kids' screen time typically increases significantly during this period.

items.title: Juneteenth: How news of the Emancipation Proclamation spread through the South
items.summary: On Juneteenth, the article explains how news of the Emancipation Proclamation spread through the South, often via informal networks and rumors, despite slaveholders deliberately withholding the information. Many enslaved people learned of their freedom while fighting was still ongoing.

items.title: These Wisconsin swing voters say Trump's war in Iran wasn't worth it
items.summary: Wisconsin swing voters participating in NPR focus groups largely view Trump's war in Iran as a "costly blunder." Their opinions highlight public sentiment regarding the conflict's value.

items.title: What you need to know about the preliminary U.S.-Iran agreement signed by Trump
items.summary:
Here's a look at the preliminary agreement between the U.S. and Iran, and the challenges that remain to find lasting peace.
(Image credit: Iranian Presidency Office via AP)

items.title: Why did the newly refurbished Reflecting Pool turn green? The quiz knows
items.summary: A news quiz features questions including why the recently refurbished Reflecting Pool turned green, along with a World Cup pun and intrigue around a tarp. It also mentions the Obama Presidential Center.

items.title: Jack Schlossberg on Trump, RFK Jr. and the future of the Democratic Party
items.summary: Jack Schlossberg discussed Donald Trump, RFK Jr., and the future trajectory of the Democratic Party. The discussion likely covered current political dynamics and potential shifts within the party.

items.title: Matt Dunlap beats DCCC-backed candidate in primary for top Maine battleground
items.summary: Matt Dunlap won the Democratic primary for Maine’s 2nd District, despite the DCCC backing an opponent, setting up a tough general election against former GOP Gov. Paul LePage. Dunlap, previously challenging the incumbent, received outside backing from a group with apparent ties to Republicans.

items.title: datasette-acl 0.6a0
items.summary: datasette-acl 0.6a0 expands Datasette's permission system from table-only access to a general resource-sharing framework. This release, largely developed by Alex Garcia, enhances granular control over who can access specific resources in multi-user Datasette instances.

items.title: Happy Juneteenth
items.summary: Slow Boring will have a reduced posting schedule in honor of Juneteenth and due to co-founder Kate's breast cancer diagnosis and upcoming surgery, though the prognosis is excellent. The site plans to publish evergreen content and Friday Mailbag posts during this period.

items.title: Thursday discussion post
items.summary: This is a general discussion post, inviting readers to engage with various topics.

items.title: AI data centers just got a government-mandated fast lane to the grid
items.summary: AI data centers have been granted a government-mandated fast lane to the electrical grid by FERC, despite the ruling not addressing existing electricity supply shortages. This decision prioritizes interconnection for high-demand AI infrastructure.

items.title: AI inference startup Baseten reportedly raising $1.5B months after its last mega-round
items.summary: AI inference startup Baseten is reportedly in the process of raising a significant $1.5 billion round at a $13 billion valuation, only months after its previous mega-round. This indicates continued strong investment in the "inference gold rush."

items.title: Almost half of US singles feel negatively about AI in dating, Match says
items.summary: A Match Group survey reveals that nearly half of US singles view AI in dating negatively, yet many dating app users are open to AI assisting with profile enhancements and conversation starters. This suggests a mixed perception of AI's role in personal relationships.

items.title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
items.summary: Amazon Web Services is reportedly in discussions to sell its AI chips to other data centers, aiming to directly challenge Nvidia and capitalize on a potential $50 billion opportunity. This move could broaden Amazon's influence in the AI hardware market.

items.title: Billionaire Ambani wants AI in every call, app, and home
items.summary: Indian billionaire Mukesh Ambani's Reliance is integrating AI into its telecom services, which are used by over 500 million people. Ambani aims to incorporate AI into every call, application, and home.

items.title: Mivo’s new app takes a mindful approach to managing screen time
items.summary: Mivo has launched a new app designed to help users mindfully manage screen time by encouraging self-awareness rather than simply forcing breaks. The app allows users to decide whether to continue using their phone, promoting conscious decision-making.

items.title: OpenAI is bringing on some big guns in the lead-up to its IPO
items.summary: OpenAI is bolstering its team ahead of its IPO by hiring prominent figures like Transformer co-inventor Noam Shazeer from Google DeepMind and former Trump AI policy official Dean Ball. These strategic hires aim to strengthen the company's position in the AI landscape.

items.title: Rivian owners file lawsuit alleging false promises on self-driving features
items.summary: Rivian owners have filed a class-action lawsuit, alleging the company made false promises for years regarding the delivery of hands-free driving features for its first-generation R1 vehicles. The plaintiffs claim they were misled about the advanced self-driving capabilities.

items.title: Snap spins off AI video team into new company, Dotmo, due to costs
items.summary: Snap is spinning off its AI video team into a new company called Dotmo due to high costs, with current Snap staff transitioning to focus solely on AI video development. This move allows Snap to reduce internal expenses while retaining involvement in advanced AI projects.

items.title: Source: Elastic agrees to buy CRV-backed DeductiveAI for up to $85M
items.summary: Elastic is reportedly acquiring DeductiveAI, a three-year-old startup specializing in using AI to detect and resolve software bugs, for up to $85 million. This acquisition reflects the growing interest in AI solutions for software development.

items.title: Telegram ban in India sparks a rush to VPNs, rival apps
items.summary: Telegram's ban in India has led to a surge in VPN use and rival app downloads, as Telegram advocates for blocking specific content rather than the entire platform. Millions of users are affected by the platform's inaccessibility.

items.title: The 11 standout startups from YC’s Demo Day, according to VCs
items.summary: VCs have highlighted 11 standout startups from Y Combinator's Spring 2026 Demo Day, with some commanding valuations exceeding $175 million. These companies represent the latest promising ventures in the startup ecosystem.

items.title: The CEO of Allbirds’ new AI biz has a plan, but no employees
items.summary: The CEO of Allbirds is launching a new AI business as a startup with a sole founder and a substantial seed round, though its future direction and employee plans are currently unclear.

items.title: The smartphone era created an attention crisis — slow tech is fixing it
items.summary: The "slow tech" movement is emerging as a solution to the attention crisis exacerbated by the smartphone era, with people seeking to regain control over their time and focus. This trend highlights a desire for intentional technology use.

items.title: The US says ASML’s top chip tool may be in China. ASML says it isn’t
items.summary: The US government claims ASML's advanced chip tools might be in China, but ASML refutes this, citing commercial logic against risking its export license. The company implies it would not jeopardize its business by arming Chinese customers with top-tier technology.

items.title: ‘Queer Eye’ life coach Karamo Brown launches Kē, a wellness app featuring his AI digital clone
items.summary: "Queer Eye" life coach Karamo Brown has launched Kē, a wellness app that features an AI digital clone of himself to guide users through personal growth. The app aims to help others with fitness, nutrition, meditation, and relationships based on Brown's own journey.

items.title: America at 250
items.summary: This article is part of "America at 250," a series counting down to the country's 250th birthday, likely featuring reflections on American history and identity.

items.title: Ayaan Hirsi Ali: Britain’s Rape Gangs and the Failure of Feminism
items.summary: A new "Rape Gang Inquiry" documents decades of "industrial-scale sexual abuse" of vulnerable white British girls by predominantly Muslim Pakistani gangs in the UK, with British feminism criticized for its silence. Elon Musk's discussions on X reportedly spurred the inquiry.

items.title: Can Courage Save America? With Rye Barcott
items.summary: Rye Barcott, co-founder of With Honor and author, discusses the meaning of courage, the value of service, and bridging political divides in America. His insights delve into what differentiates courage from bravery and the potential for military service to heal polarization.

items.title: Coleman Hughes: The Case for Progressive Patriotism
items.summary: Coleman Hughes discusses "progressive patriotism," arguing that while America's history of slavery complicates patriotism for many African Americans, their fight for freedom has ultimately made American ideals true for everyone. He critiques "The 1619 Project's" take on this.

items.title: Great Americans: The Man Who Built America’s Stonehenge
items.summary: This article profiles Harvey Fite, the visionary sculptor who single-handedly spent nearly four decades building "Opus 40" in New York, a seven-acre bluestone land art installation. The work is described as America's own "Stonehenge," harmonious with its natural environment.

items.title: How the Knicks Saved New York—and Me
items.summary: The author reflects on how the New York Knicks' NBA championship win, their first in 53 years, brought joy to over a million fans and personally provided a lifeline during a turbulent youth. The team's triumph is framed as a significant moment for both the city and the author's life.

items.title: How Vermont Became Ground Zero for the Anti-Israel Movement
items.summary: Vermont has become a focal point for the anti-Israel movement, with towns passing pledges condemning Israel as an "apartheid regime," causing distress for local Jewish residents. This trend highlights increasing polarization and discomfort over the Israeli-Palestinian conflict in unexpected communities.

items.title: Juneteenth and the Long Arc of American Freedom
items.summary: Isaac Newton Farris Jr., Martin Luther King Jr.'s nephew, reflects on Juneteenth and his uncle's words, emphasizing the holiday's dual meaning as a reminder of slavery's cruelty and America's enduring pursuit of freedom. The Emancipation Proclamation's news was deliberately delayed to enslaved people in Texas.

items.title: The Hottest New After-School Activity? Mah-Jongg.
items.summary: Mah-jongg, traditionally associated with older generations, has surged in popularity among Gen Z and millennial moms, becoming a trendy after-school activity and social event. The game's resurgence is evident in themed parties and luxury sets.

items.title: The Iran Deal Trump Can’t Legally Make
items.summary: President Trump formally signed a memorandum of understanding with Iran, pledging immediate waivers for Iranian oil exports and related services, which legal experts argue he lacks the authority to do under the Iran Nuclear Agreement Review Act (INARA). INARA requires congressional review for such deals and temporarily bars presidential sanctions relief.

items.title: Trump Blinks First in Iran, with Haviv Rettig Gur
items.summary: President Trump and Iranian president Masoud Pezeshkian signed a 14-point memorandum of understanding, initiating a 60-day negotiation period covering critical issues like the Strait of Hormuz, sanctions relief, and Iranian oil exports. This agreement is seen by some as Trump "blinking first" in relations with Iran.

items.title: Trump Got Us Here Out of Strength, Not Weakness
items.summary: The author argues that President Trump's deal with Iran, which includes a $300 billion reconstruction fund and sanctions relief for Iran's economy, signals strength rather than weakness from the US position. This perspective counters initial assessments labeling the agreement as a surrender or appeasement.

items.title: Barret Zoph is out at OpenAI again after just five months
items.summary: Barret Zoph, OpenAI's head of enterprise AI sales, has departed the company again just five months after his return. His role was significant as OpenAI focuses on enterprise as a key revenue driver ahead of its planned IPO.

items.title: T1 Phone PR firm is ‘not assisting Trump Mobile any further’
items.summary: The PR firm for Trump Mobile, Poplar Group, has ceased assisting the company, indicating continued communication issues for the media. This news comes as customers still await preordered T1 phones.

items.title: The film about Sam Altman has been dropped by Amazon MGM
items.summary: Amazon MGM has reportedly dropped "Artificial," a film about OpenAI CEO Sam Altman starring Andrew Garfield, after a year in development. The studio believes the movie, covering Altman's 2023 termination and reinstatement, would be better served by a different studio.

items.title: The NTS Radio Player brings the best of internet radio to your hi-fi
items.summary: NTS Radio and Atonemo launched a dedicated NTS Radio Player, priced at $179, bringing streaming stations and mixes to stereo systems. The device also supports popular streaming services via AirPlay 2, Google Cast, Spotify Connect, and Tidal Connect.

items.title: Valve is so behind on Steam Controller orders that some won’t ship until 2027
items.summary: Valve has announced significant delays for Steam Controller orders, with new reservations now estimating shipping in 2027 due to high demand. Despite the backlog, Valve assures customers they have no plans to discontinue the gamepad.

items.title: You can now use the Game Boy Camera with your phone
items.summary: Epilogue has released a new iOS/Android app, Flashback, allowing users to connect their Game Boy Camera to smartphones via the GB Operator dock. This updates the 1998 camera, previously adaptable as a desktop webcam, for mobile use.
